**Daniel Chester French** (April 20, 1850 – October 7, 1931) was an American [sculptor](/wiki/Sculpture "Sculpture") in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. His works include *[The Minute Man](/wiki/The_Minute_Man "The Minute Man")*, an 1874 statue in [Concord, Massachusetts](/wiki/Concord,_Massachusetts "Concord, Massachusetts"), and his [1920 monumental statue](/wiki/Statue_of_Abraham_Lincoln_(Lincoln_Memorial) "Statue of Abraham Lincoln (Lincoln Memorial)") of [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") at the [Lincoln Memorial](/wiki/Lincoln_Memorial "Lincoln Memorial") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Daniel_Chester_French&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/HBaconChesterwood.jpg/250px-HBaconChesterwood.jpg)](/wiki/File:HBaconChesterwood.jpg)

French's summer home, studio [Chesterwood](/wiki/Chesterwood_(Massachusetts) "Chesterwood (Massachusetts)") in [Stockbridge, Massachusetts](/wiki/Stockbridge,_Massachusetts "Stockbridge, Massachusetts")

French was born on April 20, 1850, in [Exeter, New Hampshire](/wiki/Exeter,_New_Hampshire "Exeter, New Hampshire"), the son of Anne Richardson (1811–1856), daughter of [William Merchant Richardson](/wiki/William_Merchant_Richardson "William Merchant Richardson") (1774–1838), chief justice of New Hampshire, and of [Henry Flagg French](/wiki/Henry_Flagg_French "Henry Flagg French") (1813–1885), a lawyer, judge, [Assistant U.S. Treasury Secretary](/wiki/United_States_Assistant_Secretary_of_the_Treasury "United States Assistant Secretary of the Treasury"), and author of a book that described the [French drain](/wiki/French_drain "French drain").[[1]](#cite_note-French,_1859-1) His siblings were Henriette Van Mater French Hollis (1839–1911), Sarah Flagg French Bartlett (1846–1883), and [William M.R. French](/wiki/William_M.R._French "William M.R. French") (1843–1914). He was the uncle of Senator [Henry F. Hollis](/wiki/Henry_F._Hollis "Henry F. Hollis").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

In 1867, French moved with his family to [Concord, Massachusetts](/wiki/Concord,_Massachusetts "Concord, Massachusetts"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) where he was a neighbor and friend of [Ralph Waldo Emerson](/wiki/Ralph_Waldo_Emerson "Ralph Waldo Emerson"), and the [Alcott family](/wiki/Alcott_family "Alcott family"). His decision to pursue sculpting was influenced by [Louisa May Alcott](/wiki/Louisa_May_Alcott "Louisa May Alcott")'s sister [Abigail May Alcott](/wiki/Abigail_May_Alcott "Abigail May Alcott").

French's early education included training in anatomy with [William Rimmer](/wiki/William_Rimmer "William Rimmer") and in drawing with [William Morris Hunt](/wiki/William_Morris_Hunt "William Morris Hunt"). French spent a year studying at the [Massachusetts Institute of Technology](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology"), and also several years in [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), Italy, studying in the studio of [Thomas Ball](/wiki/Thomas_Ball_(artist) "Thomas Ball (artist)").

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Daniel_Chester_French&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Daniel_Chester_French_in_his_New_York_studio.jpg/250px-Daniel_Chester_French_in_his_New_York_studio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Daniel_Chester_French_in_his_New_York_studio.jpg)

French in his studio with the model for Thomas Hopkins Gallaudet and Alice Cogswell, c. 1889

French first earned acclaim for *[The Minute Man](/wiki/The_Minute_Man "The Minute Man")*, commissioned by the town of [Concord, Massachusetts](/wiki/Concord,_Massachusetts "Concord, Massachusetts"), which was unveiled April 19, 1875, on the centenary of the [Battles of Lexington and Concord](/wiki/Battles_of_Lexington_and_Concord "Battles of Lexington and Concord"), the first armed conflict of the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Minute_Man%2C_Daniel_Chester_French%2C_Concord_MA.jpg/250px-Minute_Man%2C_Daniel_Chester_French%2C_Concord_MA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Minute_Man,_Daniel_Chester_French,_Concord_MA.jpg)

*[The Minute Man](/wiki/The_Minute_Man "The Minute Man")* (1874) in [Concord, Massachusetts](/wiki/Concord,_Massachusetts "Concord, Massachusetts")

French established his own studio, first in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), which he later moved to [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") and then to [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"). In 1893, French's reputation grew with his *[Statue of the Republic](/wiki/Statue_of_the_Republic "Statue of the Republic")* for the [World's Columbian Exposition](/wiki/World%27s_Columbian_Exposition "World's Columbian Exposition") in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"). Other works by French include the *[First Division Monument](/wiki/First_Division_Monument "First Division Monument")* and the *[Butt-Millet Memorial Fountain](/wiki/Butt-Millet_Memorial_Fountain "Butt-Millet Memorial Fountain")*, both in Washington, D.C., *[John Harvard](/wiki/Statue_of_John_Harvard "Statue of John Harvard")* on the campus of [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") in [Cambridge, Massachusetts](/wiki/Cambridge,_Massachusetts "Cambridge, Massachusetts"), bronze doors for the [Boston Central Library](/wiki/Boston_Central_Library "Boston Central Library") in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), and *[Four Continents](/wiki/Four_Continents_(French) "Four Continents (French)")* at the U.S. Custom House, New York, later renamed the [Alexander Hamilton US Custom House](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton_US_Custom_House "Alexander Hamilton US Custom House"). In addition to the [Lincoln Memorial](/wiki/Lincoln_Memorial "Lincoln Memorial"), French collaborated with architect [Henry Bacon](/wiki/Henry_Bacon "Henry Bacon") on memorials around the country, including the [Dupont Circle](/wiki/Dupont_Circle "Dupont Circle") fountain in Washington, D.C.

In 1893, French was a founding member of the [National Sculpture Society](/wiki/National_Sculpture_Society "National Sculpture Society"), and he was appointed a Fellow of the [American Academy of Arts and Sciences](/wiki/American_Academy_of_Arts_and_Sciences "American Academy of Arts and Sciences") in 1913.[[4]](#cite_note-AAAS-4) During this time, he served as an instructor at the [Art Students League of New York](/wiki/Art_Students_League_of_New_York "Art Students League of New York"), teaching sculpture there in 1890 and 1898.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) French also became a member of the [National Academy of Design](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Design "National Academy of Design") (1901), the [American Academy of Arts and Letters](/wiki/The_American_Academy_of_Arts_and_Letters "The American Academy of Arts and Letters") (which awarded him the Gold Medal for Sculpture in 1917), the [Architectural League](/wiki/Architectural_League "Architectural League"), and the [Accademia di San Luca](/wiki/Accademia_di_San_Luca "Accademia di San Luca"), of Rome. He was a trustee of the [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art") in New York City, and a co-founder of the [American Academy in Rome](/wiki/American_Academy_in_Rome "American Academy in Rome"). He was a Chevalier of the French [Legion of Honor](/wiki/Legion_of_Honor "Legion of Honor") and was awarded a medal of honor from the [Paris Exposition of 1900](/wiki/Paris_Exposition_of_1900 "Paris Exposition of 1900"); he also was granted honorary degrees from Dartmouth, Yale, Harvard, and Columbia universities. He was a founding member of the [U.S. Commission of Fine Arts](/wiki/U.S._Commission_of_Fine_Arts "U.S. Commission of Fine Arts"), serving from 1910 to 1915, including as chairman from 1912 to 1915.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In 1917, French and a colleague, [Henry Augustus Lukeman](/wiki/Henry_Augustus_Lukeman "Henry Augustus Lukeman"), designed the [Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize") gold medal presented to laureates. French designed the side of the prize with Benjamin Franklin on it, while Lukeman created the iconic design of the printing press and the wording on the award: "For disinterested and meritorious public service rendered by an American newspaper during the year….".[[8]](#cite_note-8) In collaboration with [Edward Clark Potter](/wiki/Edward_Clark_Potter "Edward Clark Potter") he modeled the [George Washington statue](/wiki/Equestrian_statue_of_George_Washington_(Paris) "Equestrian statue of George Washington (Paris)"), commissioned by a group that called itself "The Association of American Women for the Erection of a Statue of Washington in Paris" and unveiled in the Place d'Iena in Paris, France, in 1900; the [General Grant](/wiki/Ulysses_S_Grant "Ulysses S Grant") statue in [Fairmount Park, Philadelphia](/wiki/Fairmount_Park,_Philadelphia "Fairmount Park, Philadelphia"), commissioned by the [Association for Public Art](/wiki/Association_for_Public_Art "Association for Public Art") (formerly the Fairmount Park Art Association);[[9]](#cite_note-9) and the [equestrian statue of Joseph Hooker](/wiki/Equestrian_statue_of_Joseph_Hooker "Equestrian statue of Joseph Hooker") in Boston.

French was one of many sculptors who frequently employed [Audrey Munson](/wiki/Audrey_Munson "Audrey Munson") as a model; another frequent sitter was [Hettie Anderson](/wiki/Hettie_Anderson "Hettie Anderson"). Together with [Walter Leighton Clark](/wiki/Walter_Leighton_Clark "Walter Leighton Clark") and others, he was also one of the founders of the Berkshire Playhouse,[[10]](#cite_note-10) which later became the [Berkshire Theatre Festival](/wiki/Berkshire_Theatre_Festival "Berkshire Theatre Festival"). French's daughter, Margaret, also occasionally modeled for him, including for some of his rare portrait paintings, and became famous in her own right as a sculptor under the name [Margaret French Cresson](/wiki/Margaret_French_Cresson "Margaret French Cresson").
In 1917, [Harvard's](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") citation in conferring an honorary [Master of Arts](/wiki/Master_of_Arts "Master of Arts") referred to his statue of [Emerson](/wiki/Ralph_Waldo_Emerson "Ralph Waldo Emerson")[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*][[11]](#cite_note-HarvAlumBull_EmersonStatue-11) when it called him "a sculptor, whose skillful hand, unlike that of the friend whom he portrayed, has not been stopped but spared to adorn our land by the creation of his art".[[12]](#cite_note-Crimson1984-12)[[13]](#cite_note-HarvAlumBullv19-13) French also taught; among his pupils was the sculptor [Edith Howland](/wiki/Edith_Howland "Edith Howland").[[14]](#cite_note-HellerHeller2013-14)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Daniel_Chester_French&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Death")]

French died in [Stockbridge, Massachusetts](/wiki/Stockbridge,_Massachusetts "Stockbridge, Massachusetts"), in 1931 at age 81, and was interred in [Sleepy Hollow Cemetery, Concord](/wiki/Sleepy_Hollow_Cemetery,_Concord "Sleepy Hollow Cemetery, Concord").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Daniel_Chester_French&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Legacy")]

* [Chesterwood](/wiki/Chesterwood_(Massachusetts) "Chesterwood (Massachusetts)"), French's summer home and studio – designed by his architect friend and frequent collaborator [Henry Bacon](/wiki/Henry_Bacon "Henry Bacon") – is now a historic site owned and operated by the [National Trust for Historic Preservation](/wiki/National_Trust_for_Historic_Preservation "National Trust for Historic Preservation").[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* In 1940, French was selected as one of five artists to be honored in the 35-stamp "Famous Americans" series.[[17]](#cite_note-17)
* The [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") [Liberty Ship](/wiki/Liberty_Ship "Liberty Ship") [SS *Daniel Chester French*](/wiki/SS_Daniel_Chester_French "SS Daniel Chester French") was named in his honor.
* *[Daniel Chester French: American Sculptor](/wiki/Daniel_Chester_French:_American_Sculptor "Daniel Chester French: American Sculptor")* (2022) is a documentary film by [Eduardo Montes-Bradley](/wiki/Eduardo_Montes-Bradley "Eduardo Montes-Bradley") produced in association with [Chesterwood](/wiki/Chesterwood_(Massachusetts) "Chesterwood (Massachusetts)") and the [National Trust for Historic Preservation](/wiki/National_Trust_for_Historic_Preservation "National Trust for Historic Preservation").
* [Chester French](/wiki/Chester_French "Chester French") was an American indie band named for the artist.
